Genesis 35 shows us a man being summoned back to the place where his journey with God began. Jacob has spent twenty years away in Paddan-aram, building a family and wealth, yet God's call comes not as reward for his success but as command to remember. "Arise, go up to Bethel," God says—back to the altar, back to the place of encountering the God who met him when he was a fugitive fleeing his brother's wrath. What strikes us is that Jacob's obedience requires immediate, radical action. He demands that everyone in his household surrender their foreign gods. These aren't the gods of pagans far away; they are the household idols kept by his own family, likely including his wives. The idols are buried under an oak, hidden from sight as though Jacob is severing every visible connection to divided allegiance. This is not gentle spiritual direction. This is the work of someone who understands that returning to God means returning alone, with nothing else competing for devotion.

Yet as Jacob travels toward Bethel, God extends a protective terror over the surrounding cities—a supernatural shield that prevents pursuit. This is mercy that asks nothing of Jacob. He cannot earn it; he can only receive it. When God appears to Jacob at Bethel, the renewal is profound and particular. Jacob's name itself is changed. "Your name is Jacob," God says, "but you will no longer be called Jacob." The deceiver, the heel-grabber, is now Israel—one who has struggled with God and prevailed. More than this, God repeats the covenant made with Abraham and Isaac. The land is his. Nations will come from him. Kings will descend from him. This is no small affirmation of an aging man; this is God binding Himself again to Jacob's line through oath and direct speech. Jacob marks it with a stone pillar, anointed with oil, memorializing the place where God's presence was unmistakable and His promises were renewed.

But the narrative refuses to let us rest in this moment of blessing. Rachel dies giving birth to Benjamin, and the text offers no explanation, no divine comfort spoken over her loss. She names her son Ben-oni—"son of my sorrow"—before expiring. Jacob renames him Benjamin—"son of the right hand"—but this father's renaming cannot undo the rupture that death has torn through his family. Then we learn that Reuben, Jacob's firstborn, has slept with Bilhah, one of Jacob's concubines. The sin is recorded without elaborate judgment, but it fractures the family's structure. Jacob's twelve sons are listed, the tribes of Israel enumerated, yet they carry within them brokenness, confusion, and the marks of their father's complicated choices—Bilhah's and Zilpah's sons alongside the sons of Rachel and Leah. Finally, Jacob returns to his father Isaac, who dies old and full of years, gathered to his people. The generational promise continues, yet it moves forward through grief, failure, and human complexity that no single moment of divine blessing erases.

This chapter teaches us that encountering God is not a destination we reach and then remain undisturbed. Jacob is called back to Bethel, yes—but the road from there leads through loss and the discovery that his own household carries idolatry he did not fully recognize, that his children are capable of shame, that the woman he loves will be taken from him without warning. God's blessings are real and His covenant is sure, but they are given to us as we are—broken people moving through a broken world. The call to purify ourselves and return to God is not the end of struggle; it is the beginning of clarity about what we are actually walking through, and with whom.
